x.name: Cognition (Devin)
x.bio: Makers of Devin, the first AI software engineer. We are an applied AI lab building end-to-end software agents. Join us: https://t.co/4Ss9hvpjRG

tweets.text:
Our RL training spans four datacenters across three continents, combining our own GPUs across multiple clusters with additional compute from inference providers like @FireworksAI_HQ.

Only the trainer needs tight collective communication. Rollout inference was distributed, with engines syncing through compressed weight diffs in object storage.

Our training recipe combines many fixes simultaneously to overcome these challenges, including the Muon optimizer, staleness controls, off-policy correction, importance sampling, top-p sampling replay, MoE routing replay, and quantization-aware training.

With careful design of our optimizer, algorithm, and off-policy corrections, we find that our final config leads to higher stability and preserved entropy.

tweets.text:
For people asking why Belgium’s 86’ 0-2 comeback this WC R32 wasn’t counted was because this methodology only looks at wining without extra time.

Counting extra time didn’t feel like apples to apples to me and would include many more games. https://t.co/IRvCfTsnb6
For those curious, here are the top 5 comebacks ever in this dataset. The #1 is a comeback from Werder Bremen vs Dortmund from 0-2 at 89' to win 3-2 https://t.co/xclBW6ulh7

The Argentina come back against Egypt is one of the best matches in football history.

I analyzed ~50,000 games with Fable to show just how crazy it was.

Of the 10,138 times that a team has been up 2-0 at 79', only SIXTEEN times (0.16%) did they ever make a come back at the end of 90 minutes.

This is the first time it's happened in a World Cup.
